evidence_excerpt: Patient G.T.: maintained on daily tetrahydrobiopterin (2.4–4.9 mg/kg) with serum phenylalanine kept 42–133 µmol/L; at 2 years she was developing normally, indicating good long-term function but continued treatment requirement.

quality_of_life.evidence_excerpt: No reported neurological symptoms; neurotransmitter precursors were never required, and development was normal at 2 years while on tetrahydrobiopterin.

timeline_events.evidence_excerpt: At age 4 weeks, oral tetrahydrobiopterin (10 mg/kg) reduced serum phenylalanine from 545 to 73 µmol/L within 8 h, leading to initiation of daily BH4 therapy.
At age 7 months, BH4 was withdrawn for 2 weeks; CSF biopterin and homovanillic acid were lower and 5-HIAA subnormal, and serum phenylalanine peaked at 1390 µmol/L before decreasing.
By age 2 years, the patient was "developing normally," indicating preserved function on continued BH4 therapy.

notes_on_uncertainty: The source describes multiple patients with varying courses. This summary focuses on patient G.T., who had the clearest long-term follow-up (to 2 years). Clinical symptom details beyond developmental status are limited; symptom burden is inferred from the absence of reported neurologic symptoms and normal development while on therapy. Continued BH4 treatment indicates residual disease despite good functional outcomes.
